coredata.dc:title: Zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 encapsulating carbon nanodots and silver nanoparticles for fluorescent detection of H2O2 and glucose
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Zhenzhen
Zhu, Jinwen
Yin, Jian
Miao, Peng
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a novel fluorescent biosensor is developed for the detection of H2O2 and glucose based on Zeolitic Imidazolate Framework-8 (ZIF-8) nanocomposites. ZIF-8 encapsulating carbon nanodot (CD) exhibits bright fluorescence emission. After further loading of AgNP, the fluorescence is quenched, which is mainly based on the excited electron transfer from CD to AgNP. Besides, the excitation wavelength of CD falls within the adsorption range of AgNP, which leads to efficient inhibition of the excitation energy. The as-prepared AgNP-CD-ZIF-8 nanocomposites can be utilized as a highly sensitive platform for the analysis of H2O2 and glucose. In the presence of glucose, H2O2 can be generated by the catalysis of glucose oxidase (GOD), which induces the etching of AgNP and subsequent recovery of CD-ZIF-8 fluorescence. This “turn on” biosensor can be applied for facile and convenient quantification of H2O2. It can also be further extended to detect glucose in real samples after combining specific catalytic effect of GOD. The analytical performances are excellent, which demonstrates great potential for practical utility.
coredata.dcterms:subject: ZIF-8
Carbon nanodots
Silver nanoparticles
Hydrogen peroxide
Glucose
Fluorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of AgNP and conjugation with CD-ZIF-8
Fluorescence quenching and recovery of AgNP-CD-ZIF-8

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (Grant no. 2022YFC2403403) and the Youth Innovation Promotion Association of Chinese Academy of Sciences (Grant no. 2020324).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) FTIR spectra of CD, ZIF-8 and CD-ZIF-8. (B) UV–vis absorption spectrum of CD. (C) Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of CD. (D) Fluorescence stability of CD.
(A) EDS and (B) TEM image of AgNP-CD-ZIF-8. (C) TEM-mapping obtained for AgNP-CD-ZIF-8. Illustration of (D) Zn, (E) Ag, (F) C, (G) S, (H) N, (I) O.

(A) XRD pattern of ZIF-8, CD-ZIF-8 and simulated ZIF-8. (B) UV–vis absorption spectra of ZIF-8, CD-ZIF-8, AgNP, AgNP-ZIF-8 and AgNP-CD-ZIF-8 in the absence and presence of H2O2. (C) Fluorescence lifetime of CD-ZIF-8 and AgNP-CD-ZIF-8. (D) Fluorescence emission spectra of CD and CD-ZIF in the absence and presence of AgNP. (E) Fluorescence emission spectra of CD-ZIF-8 with the addition of various concentrations of AgNP before and after the addition of H2O2. (F) shows the corresponding relationships between fluorescence peak intensities and AgNP concentration.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of AgNP-CD-ZIF-8 after treated with H2O2 (1 mM) for different times. (B) The relationship between the fluorescence peak intensity and etching time. (C) Fluorescence emission spectra for the detection of H2O2 with a range of concentrations. (D) The relationship between H2O2 concentration and fluorescence peak intensity. Inset shows the linear range.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra for the detection of glucose with a range of concentrations. (B) The relationship between glucose concentration and fluorescence peak intensity. Inset shows the linear range. (C) Fluorescence emission spectra for the analysis of glucose, galactose, mannose, fructose, maltose, xylose, and sucrose. (D) The corresponding fluorescence peak intensities.
Illustration of the synthesis of AgNP-CD-ZIF-8 and the sensing procedure for H2O2/glucose.
Comparison of the analytical performances of recent H2O2 assays.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of the analytical performances of recent glucose assays.

row.entry: bimetallic AuRu aerogel with enzyme-like activity

head.ce:title: Zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 encapsulating carbon nanodots and silver nanoparticles for fluorescent detection of H2O2 and glucose

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Suzhou Institute of Biomedical Engineering and Technology, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Suzhou 215163, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Suzhou Institute of Biomedical Engineering and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Suzhou Institute of Biomedical Engineering and Technology, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Suzhou 215163, People’s Republic of China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Science and Technology of China, Hefei 230026, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Science and Technology of China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: University of Science and Technology of China, Hefei 230026, People’s Republic of China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Suzhou Institute of Biomedical Engineering and Technology, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Suzhou 215163, People’s Republic of China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Suzhou Institute of Biomedical Engineering and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a novel fluorescent biosensor is developed for the detection of H2O2 and glucose based on Zeolitic Imidazolate Framework-8 (ZIF-8) nanocomposites. ZIF-8 encapsulating carbon nanodot (CD) exhibits bright fluorescence emission. After further loading of AgNP, the fluorescence is quenched, which is mainly based on the excited electron transfer from CD to AgNP. Besides, the excitation wavelength of CD falls within the adsorption range of AgNP, which leads to efficient inhibition of the excitation energy. The as-prepared AgNP-CD-ZIF-8 nanocomposites can be utilized as a highly sensitive platform for the analysis of H2O2 and glucose. In the presence of glucose, H2O2 can be generated by the catalysis of glucose oxidase (GOD), which induces the etching of AgNP and subsequent recovery of CD-ZIF-8 fluorescence. This “turn on” biosensor can be applied for facile and convenient quantification of H2O2. It can also be further extended to detect glucose in real samples after combining specific catalytic effect of GOD. The analytical performances are excellent, which demonstrates great potential for practical utility.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) belongs to reactive oxygen species (ROS) family [1,2]. It acts as one of the most critical signaling molecules, which can regulate many fundamental physiological processes and cellular events [3,4]. H2O2 is generated by the catalysis of superoxide dismutase or spontaneous dismutation of superoxide [5]. Endogenous H2O2 can also be formed in the presence of certain oxidases including NADP/H oxidases and amine oxidase (AO) [6]. Besides, it participates in several metabolizations such as glucose, uric acid, and lactate [7]. Endogenous H2O2 level is usually effectively controlled by the neutralization of catalase and peroxidase. However, accumulated H2O2 induces high free radical toxicity [8]. The caused irreversible oxidative damages to lipids, proteins, and DNA are associated with many diseases like neurodegeneration, diabetes and cancer [9]. As a result, accurate determinations of H2O2 and glucose levels are highly desired and the development of novel methods is of great significance [10]. Currently, a variety of techniques have been established to monitor the concentrations of H2O2 and glucose such as CRISPR-Cas system, colorimetric, fluorescence, surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS), and electrochemical assays [11–15]. For example, Yang et al. fabricated a ratiometric SERS assay based on a pyrroloquinoline skeleton containing molecule as responsive probe [16]. Hur’s group developed a paper-based colorimetric and fluorescent strategy using N-doped carbon nanodot (CD) and metal oxide hybrid nanostructures [17]. Yao et al. investigated the enhancement of chemiluminescent reaction between H2O2 and luminol by Na2MoO4 which could be utilized for the development of highly sensitive biosensors [18]. However, there are still certain drawbacks that limit the wide applications and more efforts are needed to improve the performances of the assays.

Metal Organic Frameworks (MOFs) are a type of porous polymeric materials formed by linking metal centers with organic bridging [19,20]. They possess significant advantages of both organic and inorganic porous materials, which play important roles in the interface molecular coordination and materials science [21,22]. Many nanoscale MOFs are utilized as fluorescence quenchers and provide excellent platform for fluorescent biosensing [23,24]. Zeolitic Imidazolate Framework-8 (ZIF-8) is a simple MOF, which is usually formed by the coordination self-assembly of Zn2+ and imidazolate ligands [25]. It has aroused wide spread attention with the advantages of easy preparation, large specific surface area, good chemical stability and biocompatibility [26]. ZIF-8 is an ideal vehicle with high loading capacity for therapeutic applications [27]. However, it is rarely utilized for fluorescent biosensing due to the poor quenching property [28]. Herein, we have introduced the couple of CD and silver nanoparticles (AgNP) encapsulated in ZIF-8 for highly sensitive fluorescent analysis of H2O2 and glucose. CD is a type of zero-dimensional carbonaceous nanomaterials, which have been widely used in the fields of biosensing and bioimaging [29]. It shows unique excitation-dependent luminescence with high quantum yield (QY) [30]. The fluorescence of CD is initially inhibited after the localization of CD and AgNP in ZIF-8, which is mainly due to the quenching effect caused by AgNP [31]. In the presence of direct H2O2 or H2O2 generated by glucose under the catalysis of glucose oxidase (GOD), the oxidation reaction of silver atoms on the surface of AgNP is enhanced and the gradually dissolved AgNP can no longer quench CD in ZIF-8. Thus, the recovered fluorescence can be used to indicate the levels of H2O2 or glucose. The high intrinsic porosity of ZIF-8 results in the large surface area to encapsulate a number of nanoparticles. Thus, the high sensitivity is promised. This platform has great potential in biomolecular detection and human health monitoring.

Citric acid monohydrate, cysteine, zinc nitrate hexahydrate, 2-methylimidazole, methanol, hydrogen peroxide, silver nitrate, and GOD were purchased from Sigma (USA). Glucose, galactose, mannose, fructose, maltose, xylose, and sucrose were ordered from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (China). Human serum samples were collected from Suzhou Science & Technology Town Hospital (Suzhou, China). All protocols involving human subjects were previously approved by the Ethics Committee for the Use of Human Subjects of Suzhou Science & Technology Town Hospital (IRB2022126). All other reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification. Double deionized water purified by the use of a Milli-Q purification system (Branstead, USA) to a specific resistance of 18 MΩ cm. Fluorescence measurements were performed on a Hitachi F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded by using an Agilent Cary 660 FTIR spectrometer (Agilent Technologies, USA). Crystalline phases were determined by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker Co. Ltd, Germany). UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained using a NanoDrop OneC spectrophotometer (Thermo Scientific, USA). Transmission electron microscopic (TEM) images were taken with a JEM-2100 PLUS microscope (Japan). QY was measured by the use of an FLS1000 photoluminescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, UK).

Two g of citric acid monohydrate and 1 g of cysteine were grinded thoroughly in an agate mortar. The mixture was transferred to a beaker (10 mL) and then heated by a domestic microwave oven for 90 s. Pale brown product was obtained and dissolved in 10 mL of pure water. The suspension was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min. After removing large particles, further purification was carried out using a dialysis bag (molecular weight cut off: 500 Da). After dialyzing for 24 h, freeze-drying procedure was performed to achieve the CD powder. To construct ZIF-8, Zn(NO3)2·6H2O dissolved in 1 mL methanol (123 mM) was added into 50 mL of methanol containing 19.76 mM 2-methylimidazole. The mixture was stirred continuously for 60 min. To achieve the nanoconjugation of CD and ZIF-8, 5 mg of CD was added in 50 mL of the above solution before stirring. The nanoconjugates of CD-ZIF-8 were collected by centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 10 min, which were then washed by methanol and water, respectively. Finally, the products were dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C for 12 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of AgNP and conjugation with CD-ZIF-8
ce:section.ce:para: AgNP was synthesized by the borohydride reduction of Ag+ according to a previous report [32]. In short, AgNO3 and trisodium citrate solution with the concentration of 0.25 mM was firstly prepared. Then, 100 mL of the above solution was mixed with 3 mL of freshly prepared ice-cold NaBH4 (10 mM) with violent stirring for 30 min. The mixture was kept overnight, which was purified by centrifugation at 12000 rpm for 30 min. To achieve the conjugation of AgNP-CD-ZIF-8, 0.4 mL of AgNP (10 nM dissolved in water) was directly spiked into 0.6 mL of CD-ZIF-8 (0.8 mg/mL dissolved in water). Then, vigorous stirring was performed for 10 min.

Standard H2O2 were prepared with a series of concentrations including 5, 10, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, 120, 150, 200, 300, 500, 1000 μM. 20 μL of these samples were spiked into 980 μL of AgNP-CD-ZIF-8. After fully mixed, the solutions were incubated at 37 °C for 100 min. Afterward, the fluorescence emission spectra were recorded by a Hitachi F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer. The excitation wavelength was 360 nm and the scan range was from 380 nm to 650 nm. Other parameters included scan speed of 1200 nm/min, EX slit of 10 nm, EM slit of 5 nm, PMT voltage of 700 V.

Standard glucose solutions with a series of concentrations were prepared. 10 μL of glucose, 10 μL of GOD (10 mg/mL) and 980 μL of AgNP-CD-ZIF-8 were blended and incubated at 37 °C. After a duration of 100 min, fluorescence emission spectra were measured. For selectivity analysis, galactose, mannose, fructose, maltose, xylose, and sucrose with the concentration of 400 μM were used instead of glucose (40 μM) to yield H2O2 for AgNP etching and fluorescence recovery. For human serum assay, the samples were carefully collected, which were firstly diluted for 10 times using phosphate buffer solution and further spiked with 40 μM glucose. The following reaction procedure and fluorescence measurements were the same as described previously.

The synthesis of AgNP-CD-ZIF-8 and subsequent etching procedures are illustrated in Scheme 1 . Generally, CD is prepared via a microwave method with citric acid and cysteine as the carbon sources. The doping of N and S can benefit the improvement of QY [33]. Zn(NO3)2 and 2-methylimidazole are mixed with fluorescent CD for the assembly. ZIF-8 possesses large surface area to encapsulate CD. The formed CD-ZIF-8 nanoconjugates can be easily separated by centrifugation. AgNP can be localized via electrostatic interaction between ZIF-8 and AgNP. The formed AgNP-CD-ZIF-8 cannot emit fluorescence. The quenching process may be induced by the excited electron transfer from CD to AgNP [34]. Besides, AgNP exhibits inner filter effect. However, in the presence of H2O2, electron transfer occurs between AgNP and H2O2, hydroxyl radical and superoxide anion are generated, coupled with the dissolution of Ag(0) [35]. The quenching effect and inner filter effect of AgNP are terminated, leading to the recovery of fluorescence emission. In addition, GOD is able to catalyze the oxidation of glucose to gluconic acid by dissolved oxygen, producing H2O2 as a byproduct. Therefore, the AgNP-CD-ZIF-8 also responds to glucose. By recording the “turn on” fluorescence signal, highly sensitive H2O2 and glucose analysis can be achieved.

FTIR spectrum of CD in Fig. 1 A reflects the stretching vibrations including OH and CH (3000 to 3500 cm−1), CH2 (2940 cm−1), SH (2590 cm−1), CO (1712 cm−1), CC (1636 cm−1), CO (1662 cm−1), and CN (1018 cm−1). The peaks indicate the presence of conjugated aromatic structure together with doped S and N elements. The comparison of FTIR spectra of CD, ZIF-8 and CD-ZIF-8 demonstrates that CD can be successfully loaded on ZIF-8. In the UV–vis absorption spectrum of CD, the peak around 350 nm emerges, which is consistent with fluorescence excitation behavior (Fig. 1B). The fluorescence emission peak is excitation-independent and reflects the largest intensity with the excitation wavelength at 360 nm (Fig. 1C). The fluorescence emission of CD is also confirmed to be quite stable with laser excitation for a long time (Fig. 1D). The TEM images reveal the size and dispersibility of CD, AgNP, ZIF-8, respectively (Fig. S1A-S1C). Besides, the encapsulated CD and AgNP are evidenced in the TEM image (Fig. S1D). The conjugation of CD, AgNP and ZIF-8 can be confirmed by the energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS). As shown in Fig. 2 A, characteristic peaks of Zn, S, Ag, C, N, S, O are observed. The presence of these elements are also evidenced by TEM-mapping (Fig. 2B-2I). The XRD pattern of the prepared ZIF-8 and CD-ZIF-8 powder samples are highly crystalline. The peaks are in good agreement with the simulated ZIF-8 pattern from the single crystal data, which further demonstrate the successful synthesis of the MOF structure (Fig. 3 A).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching and recovery of AgNP-CD-ZIF-8
ce:section.ce:para: Different from commonly used MOFs with excellent fluorescence quenching ability, CD-ZIF-8 shows remarkable and stable fluorescence response in the presence of H2O2 with different concentrations (Fig. S2). The QYs of CD and CD-ZIF-8 are determined to be 50.88% and 56.81%, respectively (Fig. S3). Interestingly, the increase of QY is observed after encapsulation of CD in ZIF-8. The reason is that ZIF-8 provides an excellent scaffold to better disperse CD, inhibiting possible aggregation-caused quenching.

AgNP is utilized as the nanoquencher and direct reactant towards target H2O2 in this study. We have compared the UV–vis absorption spectra of ZIF-8, CD-ZIF-8, AgNP, AgNP-ZIF-8, and AgNP-CD-ZIF-8. As shown in Fig. 3B, AgNP-CD-ZIF-8 exhibits significant absorption at 350 nm and 400 nm, which are the UV–vis absorption peaks of CD and AgNP, respectively. However, after treated with H2O2, the curve is nearly the same as CD-ZIF-8, which indicates that AgNP is effectively dissolved. We have then studied the time-resolved fluorescence spectra of the CD-ZIF-8 in the absence and presence of AgNP. The fluorescence decay curves are shown in Fig. 3C. The fluorescence lifetimes in the absence and presence of AgNP are similar (7.5 ns and 8.2 ns). We have then compared the fluorescence emission spectra of CD before and after mixing AgNP (0.5 nM). As shown in Fig. 3D, the peak intensity decreases slight after directly mixing CD and AgNP solutions, which is due to the inner filter effect. In addition, after mixing CD-ZIF-8 and AgNP, CD and AgNP are adjacent due to the ZIF structure. With the quenching effect, the fluorescence peak is decreased remarkably, demonstrating the significance of ZIF-8 for the quenching. Next, different concentrations of AgNP are prepared for the assembly of AgNP-CD-ZIF-8. As shown in Fig. 3E, with the increase of AgNP concentration, the quenching effect is enhanced and the fluorescence emission peak decreases. After further addition of H2O2, the decreased fluorescence peak can be recovered. The detailed peak intensities and recovered intensities are summarized in Fig. 3F. It is clear that with 4 nM AgNP, the recovered fluorescence ratio is the largest, which is thus selected as the optimal concentration of the nanoquencher.

The etching time of AgNP-CD-ZIF-8 is investigated by comparing the fluorescence emission spectra. As shown in Fig. 4 A, with longer reaction time from 20 to 120 min, the fluorescence peak increases gradually. The relationship between peak intensity and reaction time is depicted in Fig. 4B. The reaction time of 100 min is selected. Under the optimal conditions, we have recorded the fluorescence emission spectra for the detection of H2O2 with a range of concentrations (from 0 to 1 mM). As expected, more H2O2 leads to more dissolved AgNP and the fluorescence emission is enhanced (Fig. 4C). The detailed relationship between the peak intensity and concentration of H2O2 is shown in Fig. 4D. The linear range is established from 5 to 100 μM. The equation is as follows:

in which y is the fluorescence peak intensity and x stands for the concentration of H2O2. The limit of detection (LOD) for H2O2 is calculated to be 0.3 μM based on the criterion of signal-to-noise ratio of 3. The performances are quite satisfactory compared with recently reported methods (Table 1 ).

Subsequently, we have prepared a series of glucose solutions to test the performances of the AgNP-CD-ZIF-8 system for glucose assay. The GOD concentration and reaction time are firstly optimized to be 100 μg/mL and 100 min, respectively (Fig. S4). Then, different concentrations of glucose from 0 to 1 mM are introduced. The recorded fluorescence emission spectra are compared in Fig. 5 A. More glucose helps the generation of more H2O2, thus the fluorescence emission is enhanced. Similarly, Fig. 5B depicts the curve of glucose concentration versus fluorescence peak intensity. Inset shows the linear range from 5 to 150 μM with the equation is as follows:
y = 974.254 + 5.717 x
n = 3 ,
R 2
= 0.998
in which y is the fluorescence peak intensity and x is the concentration of glucose. The LOD for glucose is calculated to be 1 μM based on the criterion of signal-to-noise ratio of 3. The analytical performances are superior to most glucose sensors (Table 2 ). We have further checked the selectivity of this system in the presence of other common interfering species. As shown in Fig. 5C and 5D, the fluorescence spectra for the analysis of galactose, mannose, fructose, maltose, xylose, and sucrose show no significant variation, the peak intensities of which are much lower than that of target glucose. These results confirm that this method is highly selectivity for glucose detection. To validate the practical applicability of this method, it is utilized for the measurement of glucose concentration in real samples. Human serums are firstly collected from healthy volunteers. After centrifuging and dilution, 40 μM glucose are added, which are then challenged with the proposed method. The spiking experimental results are listed in Table S1. High recoveries are obtained and the relative errors are satisfactory, confirming that the as-prepared Ag-CD-ZIF-8 nanoconjugates can be used to detect glucose precisely in real samples.

In summary, Ag-CD-ZIF-8 nanoconjugates with quenched fluorescence are assembled for highly sensitive detection of H2O2 and glucose. ZIF-8 provides excellent scaffold to support fluorescent CD and AgNP as nanoquencher. The nanoconjugates display good dispersibility and stability, which are well characterized. Fluorescence experiments manifest that target H2O2 and glucose successfully etch the nanoquencher and enhance the signal response. Wider linear ranges, low LODs, satisfactory recoveries, and high precision in real samples are achieved. Overall, the proposed method is demonstrated to be a promising platform for conventional monitoring of H2O2 and glucose. The ZIF-8 based nanoconjugation can also be further developed to encapsulate other nanomaterials for biological studies and clinical diagnosis.

Zhenzhen Guo: Conceptualization, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Jinwen Zhu: Data curation, Formal analysis, Methodology. Jian Yin: Investigation, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Peng Miao: Conceptualization, Methodology, Project administration, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (Grant no. 2022YFC2403403) and the Youth Innovation Promotion Association of Chinese Academy of Sciences (Grant no. 2020324).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2023.04.050.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
